description:
Weeks 2–3 of the HCL Java CDC program cover Backend Development.

Sections:
1. Core Java Programming — Refresher (12 Hours, Week-2)
2. Java 8 & Latest — (14 Hours, Week-2)
3. Spring IOC & Beans — (16 Hours, Week-2/3)

Mode: Instructor-led (ILT) with Hands-on
Ownership: College

Faculty:
• Core Java: Dr Rajiv Vincent — rajiv.vincent@vit.ac.in
• Java 8: Dr. RAJESH M — rajesh.m@vit.ac.in
• Spring: Dr THOMAS ABRAHAM J V — thomasabraham.jv@vit.ac.in

Goal: Build production-quality REST APIs using Spring Boot, handle data with JPA, secure with Spring Security & JWT.

Instructor-led session — Duration: 12 Hours (Week-2)
Faculty: Dr Rajiv Vincent

This is a refresher for Java fundamentals. Since you already know Java (Spring Boot background), use this to fill gaps and ensure production-level understanding.

Topics:
1. Intro to Java Platform & Language
2. JVM, JRE, JDK
3. JVM Architecture
4. Data types — Primitive, Arrays
5. Operators
6. Branching (if, switch)
7. Looping (while, for)
8. OOPs in Java
9. Classes, fields, methods, constructors
10. Keywords this, super & Modifiers
11. Interfaces & Inheritance
12. Method overloading & Overriding
13. Abstract classes
14. Packages
15. Access modifiers
16. Exception Handling
17. Collections (Comparable & Comparator)
18. File Handling
19. Memory management & Garbage collection
20. Collections Framework (List, Set, Map, Queue)
21. Generics

JAVA PLATFORM:
• Write Once, Run Anywhere (WORA) — Java bytecode runs on any JVM
• Compiled to bytecode (.class), not native machine code
• JVM interprets/JIT-compiles bytecode at runtime

JDK vs JRE vs JVM:
• JDK = Java Development Kit — compiler (javac), debugger, tools + JRE
• JRE = Java Runtime Environment — JVM + standard libraries
• JVM = Java Virtual Machine — executes bytecode

JVM, JRE, JDK:
  JDK
  └── JRE
      └── JVM (Class Loader, Execution Engine, Runtime Data Areas)

JVM ARCHITECTURE:
  Class Loader — loads .class files
  Runtime Data Areas:
    • Method Area — class-level data (static vars, method bytecode)
    • Heap — objects and arrays (GC manages this)
    • Stack — one per thread, holds stack frames (local vars, method calls)
    • PC Register — current instruction pointer per thread
    • Native Method Stack — for native (C/C++) methods
  Execution Engine:
    • Interpreter — executes bytecode line by line
    • JIT Compiler — compiles hot code paths to native machine code
    • Garbage Collector — automatic memory management

JAVA VERSIONS (LTS):
  Java 8 (2014)  — Lambdas, Streams, Optional (still widely used)
  Java 11 (2018) — LTS, HTTP Client, String methods
  Java 17 (2021) — LTS, Records, Sealed classes, Pattern matching
  Java 21 (2023) — LTS, Virtual Threads (Project Loom), Pattern matching++

HCL uses Java 8–17 range. Know Java 8 features thoroughly.

Instructor-led session — Duration: 14 Hours (Week-2)
Faculty: Dr. RAJESH M

Java 8 (2014) was the most significant Java release in a decade.
It introduced functional programming to Java.

Key Java 8 features:
1. Lambda expressions
2. Functional interfaces
3. Method references
4. Optional class
5. Streams API
6. Date & Time API (java.time)
7. Callable & Future interfaces
8. CompletableFuture & CompletionStage

Java 9–17 additions:
9. Brief intro to Java 9-17 features
10. Collectors API & Immutable Collections
11. Var keyword (Java 10)
12. File APIs
13. Switch expressions (Java 14)
14. Text blocks (Java 15)
15. Records (Java 16)
16. Logging: Log4j
17. Debugging in IDE

Streams provide declarative, pipeline-based data processing.
Does NOT modify the original collection. Lazy evaluation.

STREAM PIPELINE:
  Source → Intermediate ops (lazy) → Terminal op (triggers execution)

CREATING STREAMS:
  list.stream()                   // from Collection
  Arrays.stream(array)            // from array
  Stream.of("a", "b", "c")        // from values
  IntStream.range(1, 10)          // range
  Files.lines(path)               // from file

INTERMEDIATE OPERATIONS (return Stream — lazy):
  .filter(Predicate)              // keep matching elements
  .map(Function)                  // transform each element
  .flatMap(Function)              // flatten nested streams
  .sorted()                       // natural order
  .sorted(Comparator)             // custom order
  .distinct()                     // remove duplicates
  .limit(n)                       // first n elements
  .skip(n)                        // skip first n
  .peek(Consumer)                 // debug — doesn't consume

TERMINAL OPERATIONS (trigger execution):
  .collect(Collectors.toList())   // collect to List
  .collect(Collectors.toMap(...)) // collect to Map
  .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(Employee::getDept)) // group
  .forEach(Consumer)              // iterate
  .count()                        // number of elements
  .findFirst()                    // Optional<T>
  .anyMatch(Predicate)            // boolean
  .allMatch(Predicate)            // boolean
  .reduce(0, Integer::sum)        // fold to single value
  .toList()                       // Java 16+

EXAMPLES:
  // Names of employees in IT dept, sorted, distinct
  List<String> names = employees.stream()
    .filter(e -> e.getDept().equals("IT"))
    .map(Employee::getName)
    .sorted()
    .distinct()
    .collect(Collectors.toList());

  // Average salary by department
  Map<String, Double> avgByDept = employees.stream()
    .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(
      Employee::getDept,
      Collectors.averagingDouble(Employee::getSalary)
    ));

PARALLEL STREAMS:
  list.parallelStream()  // uses ForkJoinPool — use only for CPU-bound, large data

text: Java 9-17 Features (Records, var, Text Blocks, Switch)

description:
Instructor-led session — Duration: 16 Hours (Weeks 2–3)
Faculty: Dr THOMAS ABRAHAM J V

Spring Framework is the backbone of enterprise Java development.
HCL's entire backend stack is built on Spring Boot.

Topics:
1. Spring MVC architecture
2. Intro to Spring Boot Web (Servlet) stack
3. Spring Core — IOC & Dependency Injection
4. ORM Concepts
5. Simple REST service using Spring Web
6. RESTful API Development with Spring Boot
7. Swagger API documentation
8. Exception Handling
9. Data Access Layer with Spring Data JPA
10. Optimistic & Pessimistic locking
11. Security with Spring Security
12. Intro to Spring Cloud
13. Declarative Service-to-service communication
14. Spring Security & JWT
15. Intro to Spring Boot Reactive stack
16. Reactive Streams & Reactor
17. Data access with R2DBC
18. Spring Messaging (JMS)
19. Unit testing & Remote Debugging

text: RESTful API Development with Spring Boot
Data Access Layer with Spring Data JPA
